I am not too happy with ``connect_and_send``. This is has extremely high overhead: it does DNS lookups (modulo caching), TCP connect, TLS handshake only to send a single IQ and then abort.

If you look at how aioxmpp core handles establishing a connection (``aioxmpp.node._try_options`` and ``aioxmpp.node.connect_xmlstream``), you will notice that the error handling is also much more complex than what you currently have.

I suggest to, instead of completely re-writing the code which establishes the XML stream yourself, modify ``aioxmpp.node._try_options`` to be able to do what you need. If I’m not mistaken, your requirements are:

1. Get an XMLStream
2. Without authentication (pre-SASL)

Achieving this can be as simple as adding an ``if`` to ``_try_options`` which checks if any SASL providers are given in the security context (``metadata``). If the list is empty, simply skip sasl negotiation. You will have to modify ``node.py`` and write a few simple tests for ``_try_options``, but that’s okay. We are adding a new feature to aioxmpp core after all (which is: a way to handle pre-authentication things).


You can then replace ``connect_and_send`` with ``connect_xmlstream`` with a metadata which has an empty list of ``sasl_providers``. The usage of the IBR API would then look roughly like this:

1. ``_, xmlstream, features = connect_xmlstream()``
2. check if IBR offered in ``features``, otherwise fail early
3. use ``aioxmpp.ibr.get_registration_fields(xmlstream)`` to obtain the fields required
4. fill out the fields
5. invoke ``aioxmpp.ibr.register(xmlstream, fields)``

Good question. The hashfile contains the signature files that on Scala package not uploaded here. It follows the following format:
```Scala
abstract class SymbolAPIBase {
   /**
    * Comments
    */
   def Activation(...Args...) : Symbol
}
```
I used md5 hash in here:
```
  def MD5Generator(input : String) : String = {
    val md = MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5")
    md.update(input.getBytes("UTF-8"))
    val digest = md.digest()
    org.apache.commons.codec.binary.Base64.encodeBase64URLSafeString(digest)
  }
```
It basically take the Generated string into hash and store in this file. It should be the same across the different platform. The reason to do is is to avoid operator changes in Symbol and NDArray. One example, if one contributor changed the operators in NDArray and Symbol, he/she should comment the `require` line explained before in Scala package and update this file and also tag Scala Contributors to let them know there is a change in operator so we can determine if this will influence the user experience on Scala.

My only concern is over how to do this in a way that maintains the sanity of the interfaces through which these two consumer types communicate with each other. Adjusting private members for purposes other than their intended uses is a road I'd rather not go down.

    min_size = np.array(min_size, dtype=np.float32)

    col_power_max = math.log(max_size[1]/current_size[1], scale_step)

    row_power_min = math.log(min_size[0]/current_size[0], scale_step)
    col_power_min = math.log(min_size[1]/current_size[1], scale_step)

    mn = max(row_power_min, col_power_min)
    mx = min(row_power_max, col_power_max)

    powers = np.arange(mn, mx)

    multipliers = np.power(scale_step, powers)
    return multipliers

```
Utilities for creating PSFs from phase screens.

For PSFs drawn using real-space or Fourier methods, these utilities essentially evaluate the Fourier
optics diffraction equation:

PSF(x, y) = int( |FT(aperture(u, v) * exp(i * phase(u, v, x, y, t)))|^2, dt)

where x, y are focal plane coordinates and u, v are pupil plane coordinates.

For PSFs drawn with method='phot', an often significantly faster geometric approximation is used 
instead.  To use photon-shooting without this approximation, set `geometric_shooting=False` when 
creating the PSF.
```

The incremental mode is usually slower (see https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23736269/what-is-the-benifits-of-incremental-solving/23739636#23739636). 
In several cases it is faster to do two formulas:
```
constraints
x != v_0
check-sat
```
```
constraints
x != v_0
x != v_1
check-sat
```
Than

``` :
constraints
x != v_0
check-sat
x != v_1
check-sat
```
Specially this pattern appears every time Manticore wants to compute the PC.

However, if the formula is large and there are a considerable number of solutions, sending the whole set of constraints starts to become more costly than the cost of the incremental mode

        cluster_state2 = _ClusterState(ready)

Looking at this function, without having seen the method definition, I don't have a good idea of what `get_prob_entity` that returns `softmax_scores` is actually doing.  It's good to give methods names that give the reader a good idea of what the function does, even without having looked at the method.

Having now looked at the method, I would probably call this `_get_linking_probabilities`, `_normalize_linking_scores`, or something similar, with the returned variable just called `linking_probabilities`.  `softmax_scores` could be lots of things, as "softmax" is just a mathematical operation, not something that has semantic content.  It's better to give variables names related to their semantics, not from what operations produced them.

Also, the `linking_scores` tensor already has `batch_size`, `num_entities`, and `num_question_tokens` encoded in its size - passing those variables to this method is redundant, because you can just read them off of `linking_scores` in the method.

Lastly, I think it's more natural to have a normalized tensor like this have its last dimension be the one that's normalized.  So `(batch_size, num_question_tokens, num_entities + 1)` seems a lot more intuitive to me than how you have it.  Your need to do a transpose down below is more evidence of this to me.

> i'm a little concerned how this would look for ...  prepare a single transaction, sign it and send it raw

This is a good instinct. We want to make sure the simple things stay easy. On the other hand, we risk confusing users who don't think about nonces explicitly.

Problems
---------

If someone prepares two transactions for an account, the second will clobber the first transaction, or they will end up with a confusing error about gas price not being high enough. For example:

```
transaction_dict1 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()
transaction_dict2 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()

signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(transaction_dict1, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)

signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(transaction_dict2, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)
# ^ error or overwrite
```

Note that with the current implementation, this scenario even breaks for the client-signed approach, like:

```
transaction_dict1 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()
transaction_dict2 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()

w3.eth.sendTransaction(transaction_dict1)
w3.eth.sendTransaction(transaction_dict2)
# ^ error or overwrite
```

A Weak Solution
---------

One solution here is to add another public "pre-signing" method to always be called immediately before offline signing. Something like:

```
transaction_dict1 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()
transaction_dict2 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()

# toSignableTransaction must be called immediately before signing and sending,
# ... and called after the most recently sent transaction

signable_transaction = w3.eth.toSignableTransaction(transaction_dict1)
signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(signable_transaction, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)

signable_transaction = w3.eth.toSignableTransaction(transaction_dict2)
signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(signable_transaction, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)
```

It adds an extra step, but might save us some headaches with people who prepare transactions and don't sign/send them immediately.

`toSignableTransaction` still isn't trivial in this case, because `getTransactionCount` will not recognize the pending transactions afaik, so we'd have to be even more clever about tracking the pending transactions.

A Better Solution
----------------

Explicitly calling `getTransactionCount` is actually the best option I can see. That's because tracking nonces is so hard that we should not even try to do it automatically, we should ask the user to figure it out explicitly, like:

```
transaction_dict1 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()
transaction_dict2 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()

transaction_dict1['nonce'] = w3.eth.getTransactionCount(transaction_dict1['from'])
transaction_dict2['nonce'] = transaction_dict1['nonce'] + 1

signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(transaction_dict1, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)

signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(transaction_dict2, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)
```

Then this will work transparently again, without deleting the auto-generated nonce values:

```
transaction_dict1 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()
transaction_dict2 = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict()

w3.eth.sendTransaction(transaction_dict1)
w3.eth.sendTransaction(transaction_dict2)
```

A Stretch to get the Best of Both Worlds
-------------
Maybe we can still get a clean single solution with a special keyword, like:

```
transaction_dict = myContract.functions.myMethod().asdict({'nonce': 'current'})

signed = w3.eth.account.signTransaction(transaction_dict, private_key)
w3.eth.sendRawTransaction(signed.rawTransaction)
```

Hopefully `current` is enough of a visual signal to people that two consecutive calls will be broken. (I'm not convinced about this part, I'm going to let this marinate and come back to it)

I think I've figured out something here. What was confusing me is that, I don't believe the encoding is ever sent to the ftp server. So the server is always going to send the same data regardless of what `FTP.encoding` is set to.

I've looked at the [source](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/ftplib.py) and what appears to be happening is that its `ftplib` itself is using the encoding when it calls `self.sock.makefile` in the `connect` method. That creates a file-like object with the encoding when you connect.

So that would explain why you can't change the encoding after you have connected. But only due to a quite artificial limitation in ftplib. Otherwise you could just do a FEAT, and then set the encoding in the same connection.

It's really unfortunate.

I can think of a fudge, but its not very nice.

If the encoding is 'utf-8', you could re-*encode* the paths as 'latin-1' (effectively getting back the same bytes you received), then *decode* them as 'utf-8'. That should give you properly decode paths.

Another possibility is to do this if you detect 'utf-8' encoding:

```
ftp.file = ftp.sock.makefile('r', encoding='utf-8')
```

Both are hacks, so I'm not sure they are worth it. Otherwise we may be stuck with the single additional request to get the encoding...

You can do this with `@property` by just not providing a setter, e.g. just decorate `multiplexer` with `@property` and it should work like it does now but be accessible as `.multiplexer`.  See e.g.  https://github.com/google/styleguide/blob/gh-pages/pyguide.md#2134-decision

Also FWIW, while I understand the instinct to code defensively, ultimately in Python there's very little true defensive coding ability that doesn't rest on convention to some degree; someone can just change `_tb_context` if they want to.  And even if you've defined `multiplexer` as a method, there's no reason why somebody couldn't take your Context object and just do:

```python
context = getContext()
context.multiplexer = lambda: 42
assert context.multiplexer() == 42
```

In that respect `@property` is actually more "read-only" since you wouldn't be able to do that, at least not as easily.

I think we should be very careful about an operator like this. Previously, all of our operators were pure operations: it doesn't matter when you execute them, you can inline them into subgraphs unconditionally. `LoopIndexTensor`, on the other hand, is sensitive to the particular graph from which it is run; you can't push it into a subgraph; there is a kind of dynamic scoping going on. Maybe we will find this class of operators sufficiently useful that we will enshrine them as a class of their own but I would take a close look at it.
